names.japanese_name: フェロシアン化ナトリウム

natural_occurrence.description: Sodium ferrocyanide does not occur naturally. It is synthetically manufactured for use in salt refining and other industrial applications.

manufacturing.description: Produced by reacting ferrous sulfate with sodium cyanide and then oxidizing, or from spent oxide from gas purification. The hexacyanoferrate complex is extremely stable, with the cyanide groups tightly bound to the iron center.

other_uses.industrial: Salt refining, wine fining, photography, pigment production (Prussian blue), metal surface treatment.
other_uses.household: Present in table salt as anti-caking agent.

eu.conditions: Restricted to use in salt and salt substitutes only. Maximum levels strictly enforced.

usa.notes: Limited to 13 ppm in table salt.

japan.japanese_regulation_name: フェロシアン化ナトリウム
japan.notes: Restricted use with maximum limits in salt.

australia_nz.notes: Limited to use in salt products.

regulatory_history.event: EFSA re-evaluated ferrocyanides and confirmed safety at current use levels, though established ADI due to concerns about cyanide release under extreme conditions (not normal food use).

fun_facts: Contains CYANIDE in its name but is COMPLETELY SAFE - the cyanide is tightly locked in a stable iron complex
The cyanide groups are bonded so strongly to iron that they cannot be released under normal cooking or eating conditions
Would need temperatures over 400°C or strong acids to break down and release cyanide - never happens in food
Used in table salt for decades with an excellent safety record
Also used to make Prussian blue pigment - one of the first modern synthetic pigments (1704)
The 'yellow prussiate' name comes from its historical production method related to Prussian blue dye
Prevents salt from clumping by crystal modification rather than moisture absorption
